# Apollos AI

> Self-service portal for managing LLM API access through Microsoft Entra ID. Users authenticate with their organizational identity, get provisioned into teams based on security group memberships, and manage API keys for LiteLLM proxy access.

---

## Introduction

Apollos AI is a self-service portal for managing access to LLM APIs through a [LiteLLM](https://docs.litellm.ai/) proxy. Users authenticate with Microsoft Entra ID, get provisioned into teams based on security group memberships, and manage their own API keys, usage, and settings.

## How it works

**Authenticate**

Sign in with your Microsoft Entra ID account. The portal uses PKCE redirect flow for secure browser-based authentication.

**Get provisioned**

On first login, the portal automatically maps your Entra ID security group memberships to LiteLLM teams and generates an initial API key for each team. A progress dialog walks you through the setup and displays your keys with copy functionality.

**Use your API keys**

Use your API keys to make LLM requests through the proxy endpoint. You can create additional keys or rotate existing ones from the API Keys page.

**Monitor usage**

Track your token consumption, request counts, and costs per model and team from the portal dashboard.

## Explore


  
  
  
## Architecture

The portal consists of three components:

| Component | Technology | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Portal** (frontend) | React 19, Vite 7, TypeScript | Web UI for key management, usage, and settings |
| **API** (backend) | FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, PostgreSQL | REST API, auth validation, LiteLLM proxy integration |
| **CLI** | Click, MSAL, httpx | Command-line interface with device-code auth |

All three components authenticate against Microsoft Entra ID and interact with a LiteLLM proxy instance for LLM routing and budget management.

---

## Quickstart

## Prerequisites

**Info:** You need a Microsoft Entra ID account with membership in at least one security group that maps to a LiteLLM team. Contact your administrator if you don't have access.


## Get started

**Sign in and get provisioned**

Navigate to the Apollos AI portal and click **Sign in with Microsoft**. You'll be redirected to Microsoft's login page for authentication.

  On first login, the portal automatically provisions your account:

  1. Validates your Entra ID credentials
  2. Resolves your security group memberships
  3. Creates your LiteLLM team memberships
  4. Generates an initial API key for each team

  A progress dialog shows each step as it completes.

**Copy your API key**

After provisioning completes, the portal displays your generated API key(s) with a **Copy** button. Copy each key before clicking **Continue to Portal** — they are shown only once.

  **Warning:** Store your API key securely. You cannot retrieve it after this screen. If you lose it, go to **API Keys** and create a new one or rotate the existing key.
  

**Make your first request**

Use your API key to send a request through the LiteLLM proxy:

  ```bash
  curl -X POST https://your-litellm-proxy/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-your-api-key" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{
      "model": "gpt-4o",
      "messages": [
        {"role": "user", "content": "Hello, world!"}
      ]
    }'
  ```

  The proxy routes your request to the appropriate LLM provider based on your team's model access configuration.

  **Tip:** Go to **Models** in the sidebar and click any model card to see ready-to-use code examples in Python, curl, and TypeScript — tailored to that model's type (chat, embedding, responses, etc.).
  

**Monitor usage**

Go to **Usage** in the sidebar to track your token consumption, request counts, and costs broken down by model and team.

## Using the CLI

You can also authenticate and check your profile from the command line:

```bash
# Set up the CLI
cd cli && uv sync

# Configure your tenant and portal URL
uv run apollos configure

# Authenticate with device code flow
uv run apollos login

# Check your portal profile
uv run apollos me
```

See the [CLI overview](/cli/overview) for the full command reference.

## Next steps

---

## Development

**Info:** **Prerequisites**:
  - Python 3.12+ and [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/)
  - Node.js 24+ and npm
  - Docker and Docker Compose
  - A Microsoft Entra ID app registration (for authentication)


## Quick start with Docker

The fastest way to run the full stack locally:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/jrmatherly/apollos-portal.git
cd apollos-portal
mise run dev
```

This starts PostgreSQL, the backend API (with hot reload), the frontend (with HMR), and the docs preview server. The portal is available at `http://localhost:3000`, the API at `http://localhost:8000`, and docs at `http://localhost:3001`.

**Tip:** `mise run dev` uses Docker Compose watch mode, which automatically rebuilds containers when lockfiles change (`package.json`, `package-lock.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `uv.lock`). The `-V` flag ensures anonymous volumes (like `node_modules`) are recreated on each start so new dependencies are always picked up.


## Running services individually

### Backend

```bash
cd backend
uv sync
uv run alembic upgrade head   # Run database migrations
uv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8000
```

The API is available at `http://localhost:8000`. Swagger UI is at `/docs` and ReDoc at `/redoc`.

### Frontend

```bash
cd frontend
npm install
npm run dev
```

The frontend dev server starts at `http://localhost:3000` with hot module replacement.

### CLI

```bash
cd cli
uv sync
uv run apollos --help
```

## Environment variables

### Backend

Backend configuration is managed through the `app.config.Settings` class (Pydantic BaseSettings). Create a `.env` file in the `backend/` directory:

```bash
DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/apollos
LITELLM_BASE_URL=http://localhost:4000
LITELLM_MASTER_KEY=sk-your-litellm-master-key
AZURE_TENANT_ID=your-tenant-id
AZURE_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id
AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret
PORTAL_BASE_URL=http://localhost:3000
```

### Frontend

Frontend environment variables use the `VITE_` prefix. Create a `.env` file in the `frontend/` directory:

```bash
VITE_API_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8000
VITE_AZURE_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id
VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=your-tenant-id
VITE_LITELLM_BASE_URL=http://localhost:4000
```

`VITE_LITELLM_BASE_URL` populates the code examples shown in the **Models** page. If not set, examples display a placeholder URL.

## Running tests

```bash
# Backend tests (from project root)
uv run --package apollos-portal-backend pytest -v

# Frontend tests
cd frontend && npx vitest run

# Frontend tests in watch mode
cd frontend && npx vitest

# Frontend test coverage
cd frontend && npx vitest run --coverage

# Backend linting
uv run --package apollos-portal-backend ruff check backend/

# Frontend type checking
cd frontend && npx tsc --noEmit

# Frontend linting
cd frontend && npx biome check .

# Run all tests (backend + frontend)
mise run test

# Run all checks at once
mise run qa
```

Frontend tests use [Vitest](https://vitest.dev/) with [React Testing Library](https://testing-library.com/docs/react-testing-library/intro/) and [MSW](https://mswjs.io/) for API mocking. Test utilities are in `frontend/src/test/`.

## Database migrations

When running with Docker Compose, migrations run automatically via a dedicated `migrate` service before the backend starts. You only need to run migrations manually when developing locally without Docker.

```bash
cd backend

# Apply all migrations (local development only)
uv run alembic upgrade head

# Create a new migration
uv run alembic revision --autogenerate -m "description of change"

# Roll back one migration
uv run alembic downgrade -1
```

## Project structure

```
apollos-portal/
├── backend/           # FastAPI API server
│   ├── app/
│   │   ├── api/v1/endpoints/  # auth, keys, teams, usage, models, settings, provision, admin
│   │   ├── core/              # auth, database, graph client, teams config, logging, exceptions, rate limiting, scheduler
│   │   ├── models/            # SQLAlchemy models (users, keys, teams, audit, notifications)
│   │   ├── services/          # LiteLLM client, provisioning, key lifecycle, email, admin, audit
│   │   └── templates/email/   # Jinja2 email templates (expiry warnings, rotation, deprovisioning)
│   ├── alembic/       # Database migrations
│   └── tests/         # pytest test suite
├── frontend/          # React SPA
│   └── src/
│       ├── components/ # Shared components (Sidebar, ProtectedRoute)
│       ├── contexts/   # React contexts (AuthContext)
│       ├── hooks/      # Custom hooks (useKeys, useUsage, etc.)
│       ├── pages/      # Route pages (Dashboard, ApiKeys, Teams, etc.)
│       ├── lib/        # API client, MSAL config
│       └── test/       # Test utilities, mocks (MSW, MSAL), render helpers
├── cli/               # Click CLI
├── docker/                # Dockerfiles
├── docs/                  # This documentation site (Mintlify)
├── docker-compose.yml     # Production deployment
└── docker-compose.dev.yml # Development (bind mounts, hot reload)
```

---

## Authentication

Apollos AI uses Microsoft Entra ID for authentication across all three components (portal, API, CLI). Each component uses a different OAuth 2.0 flow optimized for its context.

## Authentication flows

**Portal (frontend):**

The portal uses **PKCE authorization code flow** via `@azure/msal-browser` v5.

  1. User clicks **Sign in with Microsoft**
  2. Browser redirects to Microsoft's authorization endpoint
  3. User authenticates and consents
  4. Browser receives an authorization code
  5. MSAL exchanges the code for access and ID tokens
  6. The portal sends the access token to the backend API with each request

**API (backend):**

The backend uses **client credentials flow** for service-to-service calls (Microsoft Graph, LiteLLM) and validates user access tokens from the frontend.

  - User tokens are validated by calling **Microsoft Graph `/me`** with the bearer token — if Graph accepts it, the token is valid
  - The `oid` (object ID) claim from the Graph response identifies the user
  - The JWT `roles` claim is extracted for admin role checks (e.g., `portal_admin`)
  - Audience verification is implicit — Graph rejects tokens not scoped to its API
  - Security group memberships are resolved via Microsoft Graph client credentials flow

**CLI:**

The CLI uses **device code flow** via the MSAL Python library.

  1. User runs `apollos login`
  2. CLI displays a URL and a code
  3. User opens the URL in a browser and enters the code
  4. After authentication, the CLI receives tokens
  5. Tokens are cached locally for subsequent commands

## Team provisioning

When a user first authenticates, the portal auto-triggers provisioning. The backend:

1. Validates their Entra ID access token
2. Queries Microsoft Graph for their security group memberships
3. Matches group IDs against the `teams.yaml` configuration
4. Creates or updates the user's LiteLLM team memberships
5. Generates an initial API key for each provisioned team
6. Returns the user's profile, team list, and generated keys

The portal shows a progress dialog during this process, then displays the generated API key(s) with copy functionality before entering the main application. This happens automatically on the `/api/v1/provision` endpoint — no manual action is required from the user.

## Token validation

The backend validates every request by:

1. Checking the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header
2. Calling Microsoft Graph `/me` with the token — a `401` from Graph means the token is invalid or expired
3. Extracting the user's `oid`, name, and email from the Graph response
4. Extracting the `roles` claim from the JWT payload for admin role checks

**Note:** The backend uses the `HTTPBearer` security scheme. Missing or invalid tokens return `401 Unauthorized`. If Graph API is unreachable (non-401 error), the backend returns `503 Service Unavailable` instead of failing silently.


## Entra ID setup

The portal requires an App Registration in Microsoft Entra ID with specific permissions and security group configuration. See the [Entra ID setup guide](/administration/entra-id-setup) for step-by-step instructions.

---

## Teams

Teams are the core organizational unit in Apollos AI. Each team maps to both a Microsoft Entra ID security group and a LiteLLM team, connecting identity management with LLM access controls.

## How teams work

A team defines:

- **Model access** — which LLM models team members can use (e.g., `gpt-4o`, `claude-sonnet-4-20250514`)
- **Budget limits** — maximum spend per team over a billing period
- **Rate limits** — requests per minute and tokens per minute caps
- **Member list** — users whose Entra ID security group memberships include this team

## Team configuration

Teams are defined in `teams.yaml` (gitignored, use `teams.yaml.example` as a template). Each entry maps an Entra ID security group to a LiteLLM team:

```yaml
teams:
  - entra_group_id: "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"
    team_alias: "Engineering AI Team"
    models:
      - "gpt-4o"
      - "gpt-4o-mini"
      - "claude-sonnet-4-20250514"
    max_budget: 500
    budget_duration: "30d"
    team_member_budget: 25
    litellm_role: "user"

  - entra_group_id: "yyyyyyyy-yyyy-yyyy-yyyy-yyyyyyyyyyyy"
    team_alias: "All AI Users"
    models: ["gpt-4o-mini"]
    max_budget: 50
    budget_duration: "30d"
    is_default: true
```

### Configuration reference

| Field                | Required | Default  | Description                                                                         |
| -------------------- | -------- | -------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `entra_group_id`     | Yes      | —        | Object ID of the Entra ID security group                                            |
| `team_alias`         | Yes      | —        | Display name for the team in LiteLLM and the portal                                 |
| `models`             | No       | `[]`     | LLM models this team can access                                                     |
| `max_budget`         | No       | `0`      | Maximum spend for the team per budget period (0 = unlimited)                        |
| `budget_duration`    | No       | `"30d"`  | Budget reset period (e.g., `"30d"`, `"7d"`)                                         |
| `team_member_budget` | No       | `0`      | Per-user budget cap within this team (0 = unlimited)                                |
| `litellm_role`       | No       | `"user"` | LiteLLM team membership role (`"user"` or `"admin"`)                                |
| `is_default`         | No       | `false`  | Mark as a fallback team — suppressed when the user qualifies for a non-default team |

### Team member roles

The `litellm_role` field controls the user's role within the LiteLLM team:

| Role    | Description                                                         |
| ------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `user`  | Standard team member — can use assigned models within budget limits |
| `admin` | Team administrator — can manage team members and settings           |

### Default (fallback) teams

A team with `is_default: true` acts as a fallback. During provisioning, if a user qualifies for at least one non-default team, default teams are suppressed — the user is only provisioned into their non-default teams. Default teams are only used when they are the user's sole option.

This prevents users in a specific team (e.g., "Engineering AI Team") from also being provisioned into a generic "All AI Users" team, which would create an unnecessary extra API key and clutter the Teams page.

**Warning:** `teams.yaml` contains Entra ID group IDs and is gitignored. Never commit this
  file. Use `teams.yaml.example` as a starting template.


### Gate groups

The optional `required_groups` field controls who can access the portal, independent of team assignment:

```yaml
required_groups:
  - "<Object ID of SG-AI-Platform-Users>"
```

If `required_groups` is set, a user must be a member of at least one listed group to sign in. If omitted, membership in any team group is sufficient. This lets you add someone to the gate group before assigning them to a team, or remove portal access without touching team groups.

## Provisioning flow

When a user signs in for the first time, provisioning auto-triggers and the portal shows a progress dialog. The backend:

1. Resolves the user's Entra ID security group memberships via Microsoft Graph
2. Matches those groups against the `teams.yaml` configuration
3. Filters out default teams if the user qualifies for any non-default team
4. Creates or updates LiteLLM team memberships for the remaining matched groups
5. Generates an initial API key for each provisioned team
6. Returns the user's teams, model access, budget information, and generated keys

Users are automatically added to or removed from teams based on their current security group memberships. Changes in Entra ID are reflected the next time the user signs in.

## Viewing teams

In the portal, go to **Teams** in the sidebar to see:

- Teams you belong to with an **Active** status badge
- Team member count — click to see the full member list with roles
- Available models per team
- Current budget usage and limits with a visual progress bar

---

## API keys

API keys authenticate your requests to the LiteLLM proxy. Each key is scoped to a specific team and inherits that team's model access, rate limits, and budget.

## Initial key

When you first sign in to the portal, provisioning automatically generates an API key for each team you're assigned to. The portal displays these keys with copy functionality before you enter the main application. Copy them at this point — they are not shown again.

## Creating additional keys

To create more keys after initial provisioning:

**Open API Keys**

In the portal, go to **API Keys** in the sidebar.

**Create a new key**

Click **Create key** and select the team to associate it with.

**Copy the key**

Copy the generated key immediately. It is displayed only once and cannot be retrieved later.

You can also create keys via the API:

```bash
curl -X POST /api/v1/keys/new \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <access-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"team_id": "engineering"}'
```

## Using keys

Include your API key in the `Authorization` header when making requests to the LiteLLM proxy:

```bash
curl -X POST https://your-litellm-proxy/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer sk-your-api-key" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "model": "gpt-4o",
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}]
  }'
```

The proxy validates the key, checks team permissions for the requested model, and routes the request to the appropriate LLM provider.

**Tip:** The **Models** page in the portal shows all models available to your teams. Click any model card to see copy-paste code examples for Python, curl, and TypeScript — each tailored to the model type (chat completions, embeddings, responses, etc.).


## Key naming

Keys are automatically named using the format `{email-prefix}-{team-name}` (e.g., `jsmith-engineering-ai-team`). If a collision occurs with an existing active key, a random suffix is appended (e.g., `jsmith-engineering-ai-team-a1b2c3d4`).

## Key status

Each key has one of the following statuses:

| Status | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `active` | Key is valid and can be used for API requests |
| `expiring_soon` | Key expires within 14 days — consider rotating |
| `expired` | Key has passed its expiration date and is no longer valid |
| `rotated` | Key was replaced by a newer key via rotation |
| `revoked` | Key was manually revoked and permanently blocked |

Keys expire automatically based on the `default_key_duration_days` setting configured for your account. Expiry is enforced at two levels: the portal's rotation cron job processes expired keys on a schedule, and the LiteLLM proxy rejects requests with expired keys at authentication time. This means an expired key stops working immediately — even before the portal's background rotation job runs.

## Managing keys

From the **API Keys** page you can:

- **View** all your keys with their status, masked secret key preview (e.g., `sk-...Ab1z`), creation date, expiration, spend, and associated team
- **Rotate** a key to expire the old key and generate a new one linked to it
- **Revoke** keys that are no longer needed

The secret key preview helps you identify which key is in use across your applications without exposing the full value.

### Rotation vs. revocation

These operations differ in how the old key is handled:

| | Rotation | Revocation |
|---|---|---|
| **Old key** | Expired (deleted from LiteLLM) | Blocked (soft-deleted, preserves spend history) |
| **New key** | Created with `rotated_from_id` linking to old key | No new key created |
| **Use case** | Scheduled credential refresh | Compromised or unused key |

After rotation, the portal displays the new key in a dialog so you can copy it. Like key creation, the new key is shown only once.

**Warning:** Both operations are irreversible. Rotation creates a replacement key automatically; revocation does not.


## Key scoping

Each key is scoped to exactly one team. If you belong to multiple teams, create separate keys for each. This lets you:

- Track usage per team independently
- Rotate keys for one team without affecting others
- Match keys to specific applications or environments

---

## Usage tracking

Apollos AI tracks usage across all LLM requests routed through the LiteLLM proxy. The usage data helps you monitor consumption, manage budgets, and understand which models your team relies on.

## What's tracked

For each request through the proxy, the following is recorded:

- **Tokens** — prompt tokens and completion tokens
- **Cost** — estimated cost based on model pricing
- **Model** — which LLM model handled the request
- **Team** — which team the API key belongs to
- **Timestamp** — when the request was made

## Viewing usage

### Portal

Go to **Usage** in the sidebar to see:

- Total token consumption over a selected time range
- Cost breakdown by model
- Request volume trends
- Per-team usage if you belong to multiple teams

### API

```bash
curl "/api/v1/usage?start_date=2026-01-01&end_date=2026-01-31" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <access-token>"
```

Date parameters must use `YYYY-MM-DD` format (e.g., `2026-01-15`). Both are optional — omitting them returns all available usage data.

The response includes per-request data points and an aggregate summary:

| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `data[].date` | string | Date of the request (`YYYY-MM-DD`) |
| `data[].model` | string | LLM model used (e.g., `gpt-4o`) |
| `data[].input_tokens` | integer | Prompt tokens consumed |
| `data[].output_tokens` | integer | Completion tokens consumed |
| `data[].spend` | float | Estimated cost for this request |
| `data[].requests` | integer | Request count (always `1` per entry) |
| `summary.total_spend` | float | Total cost across all entries |
| `summary.total_tokens` | integer | Total input + output tokens |
| `summary.total_requests` | integer | Total number of requests |

**Note:** If the LiteLLM proxy is unreachable, the endpoint returns an empty response rather than an error — usage data may be temporarily unavailable without affecting other portal functionality.


## Budget limits

Each team has a configured maximum budget. When a team approaches or reaches its budget limit:

- Requests may be throttled or rejected by the LiteLLM proxy
- The portal displays a warning on the team's usage page
- Team administrators can request a budget increase

**Note:** Budget limits are configured in `teams.yaml` by the portal administrator. Individual users cannot change team budgets.

---

## CLI overview

The Apollos AI CLI (`apollos`) provides command-line access to authentication and profile lookups. It uses Microsoft Entra ID device code flow for authentication and caches tokens locally.

## Installation

From the repository:

```bash
cd cli
uv sync
```

## Setup

Before using the CLI, configure it with your Entra ID and portal settings:

```bash
uv run apollos configure
```

You'll be prompted for:
- **Azure Tenant ID** — your Entra ID tenant
- **Azure Client ID** — the app registration client ID
- **Portal API URL** — the backend API base URL (default: `http://localhost:8000`)

Configuration is saved to `~/.apollos/config.json`.

## Authentication

```bash
# Sign in with device code flow
uv run apollos login

# Check who you're signed in as
uv run apollos whoami

# Sign out and clear cached tokens
uv run apollos logout
```

The `login` command displays a URL and code. Open the URL in your browser, enter the code, and authenticate with your Entra ID credentials. Tokens are cached locally for subsequent commands.

## Commands

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `apollos configure` | Set up Azure tenant, client ID, and portal URL |
| `apollos login` | Authenticate with device code flow |
| `apollos logout` | Clear cached authentication tokens |
| `apollos whoami` | Show the currently authenticated user |
| `apollos me` | Fetch your profile from the portal backend |

### Profile lookup

```bash
# Fetch your portal profile (teams, roles, admin status)
uv run apollos me
```

This calls the `/api/v1/me` endpoint and displays a table:

```
┌──────────────────┐
│  Portal Profile  │
├───────┬──────────┤
│ Field │ Value    │
├───────┼──────────┤
│ OID   │ abc-123  │
│ Name  │ J. Smith │
│ Email │ js@co.co │
│ Admin │ True     │
│ Roles │ portal…  │
└───────┴──────────┘
```

If the portal backend is unreachable, the CLI prints a connection error and exits. API errors (e.g., `401 Unauthorized`) display the HTTP status code and response body.

**Note:** The CLI is under active development. Additional commands for key management and usage tracking are planned.

---

## Entra ID setup

**Info:** You need **Global Administrator** or **Application Administrator** role in
  Microsoft Entra ID to complete this setup.


The portal requires a single App Registration in Entra ID that handles user authentication (browser and CLI) and backend service calls to Microsoft Graph for group membership resolution.

You also need:

- Security groups that map to LiteLLM teams
- An App Role for portal administrators
- Application-level permissions for Microsoft Graph

## Create the app registration

**Register the application**

Navigate to [Azure Portal](https://portal.azure.com) → **Microsoft Entra ID** → **App registrations** → **New registration**.

| Field                       | Value                                                                            |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Name**                    | `Apollos AI Self-Service Portal`                                                 |
| **Supported account types** | `Accounts in this organizational directory only` (single tenant)                 |
| **Redirect URI**            | Select **Single-page application (SPA)** → `http://localhost:3000/auth/callback` |

  **Warning:** The redirect URI must be under the **Single-page application** platform, not **Web**. MSAL.js uses auth code + PKCE for token redemption, which Entra ID only permits for the SPA platform. Using the Web platform causes `AADSTS9002326: Cross-origin token redemption is permitted only for the 'Single-Page Application' client-type`.
  

**Note the application identifiers**

After registration, copy these values for the portal `.env` files:

| Value                       | Environment variable                       |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| **Application (client) ID** | `AZURE_CLIENT_ID` / `VITE_AZURE_CLIENT_ID` |
| **Directory (tenant) ID**   | `AZURE_TENANT_ID` / `VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID` |


**Create a client secret**

Go to **Certificates & secrets** → **Client secrets** → **New client secret**.

| Field           | Value                    |
| --------------- | ------------------------ |
| **Description** | `apollos-portal-backend` |
| **Expires**     | `24 months`              |

Copy the secret value immediately — it is only shown once. Store it as `AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET` in the backend `.env` (or a secrets manager).

  **Note:** Set a calendar reminder to rotate this secret before it expires.
  

## Configure API permissions

The backend reads user profiles and group memberships via Microsoft Graph. The portal uses Graph API calls exclusively for group resolution — the JWT `groups` claim has a 200-group limit and silently drops groups for users in many security groups.

**Add delegated permission**

Go to **API permissions** → **Add a permission** → **Microsoft Graph** → **Delegated permissions**.

Add `User.Read` — this lets the backend read the signed-in user's profile (name, email, OID).


**Add application permission**

Go to **Add a permission** → **Microsoft Graph** → **Application permissions**.

Add `GroupMember.Read.All` — this lets the backend read group memberships for any user via client credentials flow.

  **Why application permission instead of delegated?**

Delegated `GroupMember.Read.All` requires the on-behalf-of (OBO) flow, which adds complexity and depends on the user's token having the correct scope. Application permission lets the backend call Graph API directly using its own credentials (client credentials flow), which is simpler, more reliable, and works even when the user's token has expired.
  
**Grant admin consent**

Click **Grant admin consent for [your tenant]**. Verify the status column shows **Granted** for all permissions.

| Permission             | Type        | Admin consent | Purpose                            |
| ---------------------- | ----------- | ------------- | ---------------------------------- |
| `User.Read`            | Delegated   | Not required  | User profile (name, email)         |
| `GroupMember.Read.All` | Application | Required      | Group memberships for team mapping |


**Why not the JWT groups claim?**

Enterprise users are commonly members of 200+ security groups (nested groups,
  distribution lists, dynamic groups). When the group count exceeds 200, Entra
  ID replaces the `groups` array with a `_claim_sources` overage indicator — the
  claim silently stops working. Rather than building two code paths (claim +
  Graph API fallback), the portal uses Graph API exclusively. This is the only
  reliable approach at scale.

## Configure authentication settings

Go to **Authentication** in your App Registration.

**Set redirect URIs**

Under **Single-page application** → **Redirect URIs**, add:

```
http://localhost:3000/auth/callback          (development)
https://ai-portal.company.com/auth/callback  (production)
```


**Disable implicit grant**

Under **Implicit grant and hybrid flows**, leave both unchecked:
  - Access tokens — unchecked
  - ID tokens — unchecked

The portal uses auth code + PKCE, not implicit flow.


**Enable public client flows (for CLI)**

Under **Advanced settings**, set **Allow public client flows** → **Yes**.

This is required for the CLI's MSAL device-code flow. If you don't plan to use the CLI, leave this as **No**.


## Create app roles

App Roles control who is a portal administrator versus a regular user.

Go to **App roles** → **Create app role**:

| Field                    | Value                                                              |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Display name**         | `Portal Administrator`                                             |
| **Allowed member types** | `Users/Groups`                                                     |
| **Value**                | `portal_admin`                                                     |
| **Description**          | `Full administrative access to the Apollos AI provisioning portal` |
| **Enable this app role** | Checked                                                            |

**Note:** Regular portal users don't need an explicit role — security group membership
  is sufficient for access. The `portal_admin` role grants access to the admin
  dashboard, audit logs, and user management.


## Set up security groups

### Create team groups

Create a security group for each LiteLLM team. Go to **Microsoft Entra ID** → **Groups** → **New group**:

| Field                 | Value                                                         |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Group type**        | Security                                                      |
| **Group name**        | `SG-AI-Engineering-Team` (your naming convention)             |
| **Group description** | `Members provisioned into the Engineering AI Team on LiteLLM` |
| **Membership type**   | Assigned (or Dynamic for attribute-based membership)          |

Note the **Object ID** of each group — these go into the portal's `teams.yaml`:

```yaml
teams:
  - entra_group_id: "<Object ID from Azure>"
    team_alias: "Engineering AI Team"
    models: ["gpt-4o", "gpt-4o-mini"]
    max_budget: 500
    budget_duration: "30d"
    team_member_budget: 25
    litellm_role: "user"

  # Optional: a default team suppressed when the user has a non-default team
  - entra_group_id: "<Object ID of SG-AI-Platform-Users>"
    team_alias: "All AI Users"
    models: ["gpt-4o-mini"]
    max_budget: 50
    budget_duration: "30d"
    is_default: true
```

### Create a gate group (recommended)

Create a separate "gate" security group that controls who can access the portal, independent of team assignment:

| Group                    | Purpose                                      |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------- |
| `SG-AI-Platform-Users`   | Must be a member to access the portal        |
| `SG-AI-Engineering-Team` | Determines which LiteLLM team the user joins |

A user must be in the gate group **and** at least one team group. This lets you:

- Add someone to `SG-AI-Platform-Users` without immediately assigning a team
- Remove someone from `SG-AI-Platform-Users` to block all portal access without touching team groups

Configure in `teams.yaml`:

```yaml
required_groups:
  - "<Object ID of SG-AI-Platform-Users>"
```

## Assign groups to the enterprise application

The App Registration creates a corresponding Enterprise Application. Go to **Microsoft Entra ID** → **Enterprise applications** → find your portal app → **Users and groups** → **Add user/group**.

| Group                    | Role                 | Purpose                                     |
| ------------------------ | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `SG-AI-Platform-Users`   | User (default)       | All users who can access the portal         |
| `SG-AI-Platform-Admins`  | Portal Administrator | Portal admins (maps to `portal_admin` role) |
| `SG-AI-Engineering-Team` | User                 | Maps to Engineering AI Team in LiteLLM      |
| `SG-AI-DataScience-Team` | User                 | Maps to Data Science Team in LiteLLM        |

**Tip:** Under **Properties**, set **Assignment required?** → **Yes** to prevent users
  not assigned to the Enterprise App from signing in. This is recommended for
  controlled rollout.


## Environment variables

After setup, add these to your portal environment:

```bash backend/.env
AZURE_TENANT_ID=<Directory (tenant) ID>
AZURE_CLIENT_ID=<Application (client) ID>
AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET=<Client secret value>
PORTAL_ADMIN_ROLE=portal_admin
```

```bash frontend/.env
VITE_AZURE_CLIENT_ID=<Application (client) ID>
VITE_AZURE_TENANT_ID=<Directory (tenant) ID>
```

## Verify the setup

### Checklist

- [ ] App Registration created with SPA redirect URIs
- [ ] Client secret generated and stored securely
- [ ] Delegated permission `User.Read` added with admin consent
- [ ] Application permission `GroupMember.Read.All` added with admin consent
- [ ] Public client flows enabled (if CLI is needed)
- [ ] App Role `portal_admin` created
- [ ] Enterprise Application: **Assignment required** = Yes
- [ ] Security groups created for each LiteLLM team
- [ ] Gate group created (e.g., `SG-AI-Platform-Users`)
- [ ] All groups assigned to the Enterprise Application
- [ ] Admin group assigned with Portal Administrator role

### Test the auth flow

**Test user authentication (device-code flow)**

```bash
  # Request a device code
  curl -X POST "https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/oauth2/v2.0/devicecode" \
    -d "client_id=<client-id>" \
    -d "scope=openid profile email User.Read"

# Follow the instructions: visit https://microsoft.com/devicelogin and enter the code

# Poll for the token

curl -X POST "https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/oauth2/v2.0/token" \
 -d "client_id=<client-id>" \
 -d "grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant_type:device_code" \
 -d "device_code=<device_code_from_above>"

````

Decode the ID token at [jwt.ms](https://jwt.ms) and verify:
- `oid` is present (user Object ID)
- `preferred_username` has the email
- `roles` contains `portal_admin` (if the user has that role)
- `groups` is **not** in the token — this is expected (the portal uses Graph API)

**Test Graph API group resolution (client credentials)**

```bash
# Get an app-only token
curl -X POST "https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/oauth2/v2.0/token" \
  -d "client_id=<client-id>" \
  -d "client_secret=<client-secret>" \
  -d "scope=https://graph.microsoft.com/.default" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials"

# List group memberships for a user (by their Entra OID)
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" \
  "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users/<user-oid>/memberOf/microsoft.graph.group?\$select=id,displayName&\$top=999"
````

The response should list all groups the user belongs to. Match the group `id` values against the `entra_group_id` entries in your `teams.yaml`.

  **Note:** Graph API returns a maximum of 999 results per page. If the response includes `@odata.nextLink`, follow it to get the complete list. The portal backend handles pagination automatically.
  


## Ongoing maintenance

| Task                         | Frequency                 | Details                                                                                                        |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Rotate client secret         | Before expiry (24 months) | Create a new secret → update portal env → delete old secret                                                    |
| Review group memberships     | As needed                 | Add or remove users from security groups. The portal picks up changes on next login or deprovisioning cron run |
| Audit admin role assignments | Quarterly                 | Review who has the `Portal Administrator` role in the Enterprise Application                                   |
| Monitor consent status       | After policy changes      | Verify admin consent is still granted for `GroupMember.Read.All` after conditional access changes              |

## Troubleshooting

| Error                                                       | Cause                                       | Fix                                                                        |
| ----------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `AADSTS65001: The user or admin has not consented`          | Admin consent not granted                   | Go to API permissions → Grant admin consent                                |
| `AADSTS700016: Application not found in tenant`             | Wrong client or tenant ID                   | Verify `AZURE_CLIENT_ID` and `AZURE_TENANT_ID` match your App Registration |
| `AADSTS50105: The signed in user is not assigned to a role` | User not assigned to Enterprise Application | Add the user's group to the Enterprise App                                 |
| Graph API returns 403 on `/memberOf`                        | Missing `GroupMember.Read.All` permission   | Grant admin consent for the application permission                         |
| Graph API returns partial group list                        | Pagination not followed                     | Ensure the backend follows all `@odata.nextLink` pages                     |
| `roles` claim missing from token                            | User not assigned an App Role               | Check Enterprise App → Users and groups → Role column                      |
| Device-code flow fails                                      | Public client flows not enabled             | Set **Allow public client flows** → **Yes** in Authentication              |
| `AADSTS9002326: Cross-origin token redemption`              | Redirect URI under Web platform, not SPA    | Move redirect URI to the Single-page application platform                  |

---

## API reference

The Apollos AI backend exposes a REST API at `/api/v1/` for managing authentication, provisioning, API keys, teams, usage, models, and settings.

## Base URL

```
http://localhost:8000/api/v1
```

In production, replace with your deployed backend URL.

## Authentication

All endpoints (except `/health` and `/ready`) require a valid Microsoft Entra ID access token in the `Authorization` header:

```bash
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <access-token>" \
  https://your-backend/api/v1/me
```

## Endpoints

### User endpoints

| Endpoint | Method | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `/api/v1/me` | GET | Get the current user's profile (OID, email, name, roles, admin status) |
| `/api/v1/status` | GET | Check provisioning status |
| `/api/v1/provision` | POST | Provision the current user (map Entra ID groups to LiteLLM teams) |
| `/api/v1/keys` | GET | List the current user's API keys |
| `/api/v1/keys/new` | POST | Create a new API key |
| `/api/v1/keys/{key_id}/rotate` | POST | Rotate an API key |
| `/api/v1/keys/{key_id}/revoke` | POST | Revoke an API key |
| `/api/v1/teams` | GET | List the current user's teams |
| `/api/v1/usage` | GET | Get usage statistics |
| `/api/v1/models` | GET | List available models for the user's teams |
| `/api/v1/settings` | GET | Get user settings |
| `/api/v1/settings` | PATCH | Update user settings |

### Admin endpoints

Admin endpoints require the `portal_admin` role in Entra ID app roles.

| Endpoint | Method | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `/api/v1/admin/users` | GET | List all provisioned users (paginated) |
| `/api/v1/admin/users/{user_id}` | GET | Get detailed user info with keys, teams, and audit history |
| `/api/v1/admin/users/{user_id}/deprovision` | POST | Deprovision a user (block keys, mark inactive) |
| `/api/v1/admin/users/{user_id}/reprovision` | POST | Re-activate a deprovisioned user |
| `/api/v1/admin/keys` | GET | List all active keys across users (paginated) |
| `/api/v1/admin/keys/{key_id}/revoke` | POST | Admin-revoke any key by ID |
| `/api/v1/admin/audit` | GET | Query audit log with filters and pagination |
| `/api/v1/admin/audit/export` | GET | Export audit log as CSV (max 10,000 rows) |
| `/api/v1/admin/health` | GET | Get scheduler status and background job info |

### Health checks

Two unauthenticated health endpoints are available at the root:

| Endpoint | Description |
|---|---|
| `/health` | Basic liveness check |
| `/ready` | Readiness check (database and LiteLLM connectivity) |

## OpenAPI spec

The backend auto-generates an OpenAPI specification. When running locally:

- **Swagger UI**: `http://localhost:8000/docs`
- **ReDoc**: `http://localhost:8000/redoc`
- **Raw spec**: `http://localhost:8000/openapi.json`

---

## Contributing

Contributions to Apollos AI are welcome. This guide covers how to submit changes to both the codebase and documentation.

## Prerequisites

| Tool | Version | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Python | 3.12+ | Backend and CLI |
| uv | Latest | Python package management |
| Node.js | 24+ | Frontend |
| Docker | Latest | PostgreSQL and full-stack dev |
| mise | Latest | Task runner (optional) |

See the [development guide](/development) for full setup instructions, environment variables, and project structure.

## Code contributions

### Getting started

1. Fork and clone the repository
2. Follow the [development setup](/development) to get your local environment running
3. Create a branch for your changes
4. Make changes and run the test suite
5. Submit a pull request

### Pre-commit checks

Run all checks before submitting a pull request:

```bash
mise run qa
```

This runs backend tests (pytest), frontend tests (vitest), linting (ruff + biome), and type checking (tsc).

### Code style

- **Python**: Ruff handles linting and formatting. Run `uv run --package apollos-portal-backend ruff check --fix backend/` to auto-fix
- **TypeScript**: Biome handles linting and formatting. Run `cd frontend && npx biome check --write .` to auto-fix
- **Commits**: Use conventional commit messages (`feat:`, `fix:`, `chore:`, `docs:`)

### Repository structure

The project uses a uv workspace with three packages:

- `backend/` — `apollos-portal-backend` (FastAPI + SQLAlchemy)
- `cli/` — `apollos-cli` (Click CLI with MSAL device-code auth)
- Root `pyproject.toml` — workspace configuration and shared ruff settings

### Claude Code (optional)

If you use [Claude Code](/ai-tools/claude-code), copy the MCP server configuration:

```bash
cp .mcp.json.example .mcp.json
```

This enables context7 (live documentation lookup), Playwright (browser automation), and GitHub (PR/issue management) MCP servers. The GitHub server requires a Personal Access Token — see the [Claude Code setup guide](/ai-tools/claude-code) for configuration details and available skills.

## Documentation contributions

### Option 1: edit on GitHub

Navigate to the page you want to edit in the `docs/` directory and click the pencil icon to edit directly on GitHub.

### Option 2: local development

1. Install the Mintlify CLI: `npm i -g mint`
2. Navigate to the `docs/` directory
3. Run `mint dev` to preview at `http://localhost:3000`
4. Make your changes — the preview updates automatically
5. Run `mint broken-links` to check for broken links
6. Submit a pull request

### Writing guidelines

- Use active voice and second person ("you")
- Keep sentences concise — one idea per sentence
- Use sentence case for headings
- Bold for UI elements: Click **Settings**
- Code formatting for file names, commands, and code references
- See `docs/AGENTS.md` for full terminology and style conventions

## Entra ID app registration

To test authentication locally, you need a Microsoft Entra ID app registration with:

- **Redirect URI**: `http://localhost:3000/auth/callback` (SPA type)
- **API permissions**: `User.Read` (delegated), `GroupMember.Read.All` (application)
- **Client secret** for backend service-to-service calls

Set the corresponding environment variables in `backend/.env` and `frontend/.env` as described in the [development guide](/development).

---

## Claude Code setup

Set up Claude Code to work with the Apollos AI codebase, including MCP servers, skills, and documentation tools.

## Prerequisites

- Active Claude subscription (Pro, Max, or API access)
- Node.js 24+ (for MCP servers and frontend tooling)

## Setup

**Install Claude Code**

```bash
  npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
  ```

**Configure MCP servers**

Copy the example MCP configuration to enable project-level MCP servers:

  ```bash
  cp .mcp.json.example .mcp.json
  ```

  The example configuration includes three MCP servers. Edit `.mcp.json` to add your GitHub PAT for the GitHub server, or remove servers you don't need.

  **Note:** `.mcp.json` is gitignored. You can customize your local copy without affecting other contributors.
  

**Start working**

```bash
  claude
  ```

  The project includes built-in skills, subagents, and hooks in the `.claude/` directory. These are available automatically.

## MCP servers

The `.mcp.json.example` file configures these servers:

| Server | Purpose | Setup |
|--------|---------|-------|
| [context7](https://github.com/upstash/context7) | Live documentation lookup for project dependencies (FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, React, MSAL, etc.) | Works out of the box |
| [Playwright](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-mcp-playwright) | Browser automation for visual testing and E2E verification of frontend flows | Works out of the box |
| [GitHub](https://github.com/anthropics/anthropic-mcp-github) | Structured access to issues, pull requests, and Actions status | Requires `GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN` in `.mcp.json` |

**context7:**

Provides live documentation for any library in the project's dependency tree. Ask questions like "how does FastAPI dependency injection work?" and Claude will fetch current docs instead of relying on training data.

  No configuration required.

**Playwright:**

Enables browser automation for testing frontend flows visually. Useful for verifying:
  - The auto-provisioning progress dialog and key display
  - Responsive layout across screen sizes
  - Copy-to-clipboard behavior
  - Animation and transition rendering

  No configuration required — launches a headless browser on demand.

**GitHub:**

Provides structured access to the `jrmatherly/apollos-portal` repository without shell commands. Supports:
  - Viewing and creating pull requests
  - Reading and commenting on issues
  - Checking CI/Actions status

  Requires a GitHub Personal Access Token with `repo` scope:

  ```json
  "github": {
    "command": "npx",
    "args": ["-y", "@anthropic/mcp-github"],
    "env": {
      "GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN": "ghp_your_token_here"
    }
  }
  ```

## Skills

| Skill | Command | Purpose |
|-------|---------|---------|
| API doc sync | `/api-doc` | Sync endpoint docs with backend routes |
| New endpoint | `/new-endpoint` | Scaffold a FastAPI endpoint with tests |
| Pre-commit check | `/precheck` | Run full QA suite (tests, lint, types) |
| Migration | `/migration` | Generate and apply Alembic migrations |
| Generate test | `/gen-test` | Scaffold a Vitest + RTL test file for a frontend component |
| Dependency check | `/deps-check` | Audit Python and Node dependencies for updates and vulnerabilities |

## Subagents

| Agent | Purpose |
|-------|---------|
| `security-reviewer` | OWASP-focused security review (auth, CORS, injection, CSV) |
| `docs-reviewer` | Cross-reference docs against code for accuracy |
| `provisioning-tester` | Validate consistency across the provisioning pipeline (backend, frontend, tests, config, docs) |

## Hooks

Hooks run automatically on file edits:

| Hook | Trigger | Behavior |
|------|---------|----------|
| Auto-format | PostToolUse (Edit/Write) | Formats Python (ruff) and TypeScript/MDX (biome) on save |
| Related test runner | PostToolUse (Edit/Write) | Runs co-located `.test.tsx` file when a source `.tsx` file is edited |
| File protection | PreToolUse (Edit/Write) | Blocks edits to `.env`, `teams.yaml`, and lock files |

## Documentation workflow

To contribute to this documentation site using Claude Code:

1. Install the [Mintlify skill](https://mintlify.com/docs/mcp) for component reference and writing standards
2. Read `docs/AGENTS.md` for project-specific terminology and style guide
3. Preview changes with `cd docs && mint dev`
4. Validate before committing with `cd docs && mint validate && mint broken-links`

---

## Cursor setup

Set up Cursor to help you write and maintain your Mintlify documentation.

## Prerequisites

- Cursor editor installed

## Setup

**Open your docs directory in Cursor**

Open the root of your documentation repository where `docs.json` is located.

**Install the Mintlify skill**

In the integrated terminal, run:

  ```bash
  npx skills add https://mintlify.com/docs
  ```

  This gives Cursor Mintlify's component reference, writing standards,
  and workflow guidance.

**Customize project instructions**

Edit `AGENTS.md` in your project root to add project-specific terminology,
  style preferences, and content boundaries.

**Start writing with Cursor**

Open a file and use Cursor's AI features to draft and edit documentation.

---

## Windsurf setup

Set up Windsurf's Cascade AI assistant to help you write and maintain your Mintlify documentation.

## Prerequisites

- Windsurf editor installed

## Setup

**Open your docs directory in Windsurf**

Open the root of your documentation repository where `docs.json` is located.

**Install the Mintlify skill**

In the integrated terminal, run:

  ```bash
  npx skills add https://mintlify.com/docs
  ```

  This gives Windsurf Mintlify's component reference, writing standards,
  and workflow guidance.

**Customize project instructions**

Edit `AGENTS.md` in your project root to add project-specific terminology,
  style preferences, and content boundaries.

**Start writing with Cascade**

Open a file and use Cascade to draft and edit documentation.
